title: Being attracted to your therapist is more common than you think

paragraphs.english: Being attracted to your therapist is more common than you think
paragraphs.pidgin: Being attracted to your therapist na more common than you think

paragraphs.english: Virtual intimacy platform Dating.com found that of the 1,000 people surveyed, nearly half (47 per cent) admitted to feeling an attraction toward their therapist, while more than a quarter (31 per cent) confessed to having intimate dreams about them. GETTY IMAGES
paragraphs.pidgin: Virtual intimacy platform Dating.com find tok of di 1,000 pipul surveyed, nearly half (47 per cent) admitted to feeling an attraction toward dem therapist, while more than a quarter (31 per cent) confessed to having intimate dreams about dem. GETTY IMAGES

paragraphs.english: If you’ve ever been attracted to your therapist, you’re not alone.
paragraphs.pidgin: If you’ve ever dey attracted to your therapist, you’re no alone.

paragraphs.english: A new study found that more and more people are drawn to their therapist with some even admitting that they’ve started dating theirs.
paragraphs.pidgin: A new study find tok more more pipul dey drawn to dem therapist wit some even admitting dat dem’ve start dating dem own.

paragraphs.english: As the stigma surrounding mental health becomes increasingly fragmented, therapy has become more common. But as people open up about some of their deepest thoughts and feelings, that line between therapist and confidante can get blurred.
paragraphs.pidgin: As di stigma surrounding mental health becomes increasingly fragmented, therapy get turn more common. But as pipul open up about some of dem deepest thoughts and feelings, dat line between therapist and confidante fit get blurred.

paragraphs.english: Virtual intimacy platform Dating.com found that of the 1,000 people surveyed, nearly half (47 per cent) admitted to feeling an attraction toward their therapist, while more than a quarter (31 per cent) confessed to having intimate dreams about them. As well, 28 per cent took it a step further and revealed they had either dated or tried to date their therapist with 57 per cent believing it was totally acceptable.
paragraphs.pidgin: Virtual intimacy platform Dating.com find tok of di 1,000 pipul surveyed, nearly half (47 per cent) admitted to feeling an attraction toward dem therapist, while more than a quarter (31 per cent) confessed to having intimate dreams about dem. As well, 28 per cent take e a step further and revealed dem bin get either dated or try to date dem therapist wit 57 per cent believing e bin na totally acceptable.

paragraphs.english: An analysis of Google Trends found that searches for “I love my therapist” and “my therapist is hot” had surged by 181 per cent and 134 per cent, respectively, over the past month. Meanwhile, searches for “dating my therapist” were up a whopping 324 per cent, according to the data.
paragraphs.pidgin: An analysis of Google Trends find tok searches for “I love my therapist” and “my therapist na hot” bin $2 by 181 per cent and 134 per cent, respectively, over di past month. Meanwhile, searches for “dating my therapist” bin dey up a whopping 324 per cent, as di data.

paragraphs.english: “Therapy is impossible without a genuine connection, trust and showing your true emotions, so it’s not surprising that romantic feelings may appear,” said Jaime Bronstein, Dating.com’s resident therapist.
paragraphs.pidgin: “Therapy na impossible without a genuine connection, trust and showing your true emotions, so e’s no surprising dat romantic feelings may appear,” tok Jaime Bronstein, Dating.com’s resident therapist.

paragraphs.english: “However, you should acknowledge your feelings to your therapist instead of hiding them.
paragraphs.pidgin: “but, you suppose to acknowledge your feelings to your therapist instead of hiding dem.

paragraphs.english: “Bringing this attraction into the session allows you to understand your attachment patterns, turning an awkward crush into a breakthrough in emotional intelligence.”
paragraphs.pidgin: “Bringing dis attraction into di session allows you to understand your attachment patterns, turning an awkward crush into a breakthrough for emotional intelligence.”

paragraphs.english: Bronstein noted that a professional therapist would neither be flustered nor take it personally.
paragraphs.pidgin: Bronstein note tok a professional therapist go neither be flustered nor take e personally.

paragraphs.english: On the contrary, she pointed out that if a client develops a romantic affection, it should be acknowledged and their emotions should be validated—all while maintaining a professional, one-sided approach and simply explaining a therapist’s code of ethics.
paragraphs.pidgin: On di contrary, im talk about dat if a client develops a romantic affection, e suppose to be acknowledged and dem emotions suppose to be validated—all while maintaining a professional, one-sided approach and simply explaining a therapist’s code of ethics.

paragraphs.english: “The right course of action would be to treat these feelings as data for analyzing a patient’s attachment pattern. Typically, we start with a discussion,” she said.
paragraphs.pidgin: “Di correct course of action go be to treat dis ones feelings as data for analyzing a patient’s attachment pattern. Typically, we start wit a discussion,” im tok.

paragraphs.english: “By discussing these feelings, the therapist can understand if it’s a recurring issue for a client and discover the root cause, which often lies in past relationships.
paragraphs.pidgin: “By discussing dis ones feelings, di therapist fit understand if e’s a recurring wahala for a client and discover di root cause, which often lies for past relationships.

paragraphs.english: “If the client is obsessing over you instead of working on their mental health, it’s called ‘eroticized transference.’ A deep dive into the client’s past attachments is necessary to break the cycle.”
paragraphs.pidgin: “If di client dey obses over you instead of working on dem mental health, e’s call ‘eroticized transference.’ A deep dive into di client’s past attachments na necessary to break di cycle.”

paragraphs.english: Bronstein recounted how one male patient stopped seeing her after a few visits because he thought she was too attractive and “couldn’t concentrate on the sessions.”
paragraphs.pidgin: Bronstein recounted how one male patient stop seeing im after a small small visits because im think im bin dey too attractive and “couldn’t concentrate on di sessions.”

paragraphs.english: She admitted that she was flattered and found it “endearing,” and while she has worked with some men with flirty personalities, she said she has “always remained professional”—which is key, Bronstein insisted.
paragraphs.pidgin: Im admitted dat im bin dey flattered and find e “endearing,” and while im don work wit some men wit flirty personalities, im tok im get “always remained professional”—which na main, Bronstein insisted.

paragraphs.english: But if things take a turn, it may be better for them to seek help elsewhere.
paragraphs.pidgin: But if things take a turn, e may be better for dem to seek help elsewhere.

paragraphs.english: “Referring the client to another specialist could be an exit strategy if the client becomes overly obsessed or if the therapist starts to like the person back,” Bronstein said. “In both cases, referring the client to a colleague is the only way to protect the client’s mental health.”
paragraphs.pidgin: “Referring di client to another specialist fit be an exit strategy if di client becomes overly obsessed or if di therapist starts to like di person back,” Bronstein tok. “For both cases, referring di client to a colleague na di only way to protect di client’s mental health.”

paragraphs.english: This article was originally published in the Toronto Sun on March 8, 2026.
paragraphs.pidgin: Dis article bin originally published for di Toronto Sun on March 8, 2026.
